titlestmt.titleproper: Guide to the Rosanna Warren Papers
titlestmt.author: By Michael L. Forstrom

publicationstmt.publisher: Beinecke Rare Book and Manuscript Library

maintenanceagency.agencyname: Beinecke Rare Book and Manuscript Library

descriptivenote.p: Finding aid description and metadata in Archives at Yale are licensed under a CC0 1.0 Universal (CC0 1.0) Public Domain Dedication. This means that anyone may copy, modify, and distribute this descriptive metadata without restriction or permission. The CC0 license is explained in full on the Creative Commons website: CC0 1.0 Universal (CC0 1.0) Public Domain Dedication. More information is available on our Help page.

maintenanceevent.eventdescription: This finding aid was produced using ArchivesSpace on Thursday January 9, 2025 at 01:00

corpname.part: Beinecke Rare Book and Manuscript Library

accessrestrict.head: Conditions Governing Access
accessrestrict.p: This collection is open for research.
Box 79 (optical disks): Restricted fragile material. Reference copies may be requested. Consult Access Services for further information.

arrangement.head: Arrangement
arrangement.p: Organized into three series: I. Subject Files, [1894?]-2023. II. Writings, 1960s-2022. III. Personal Papers, 1892-2022.

bioghist.head: Biographical / Historical
bioghist.p: Rosanna Warren (born July 27, 1953), an American poet and educator, is the daughter of authors Eleanor Clark and Robert Penn Warren. She received a B.A. in painting from Yale University in 1976 and an M.A. from Johns Hopkins University in 1980. She is the author of several collections of poetry, a book of literary criticism, a biography, and translations. Her works include: Snow day (Palaemon Press, 1981), Each leaf shines separate (Norton, 1984), Stained glass (Norton, 1993), Suppliant women (Oxford University Press, 1995), Departure (Norton, 2003), Fables of the self: Studies in lyric poetry (Norton, 2008), Ghost in a red hat (Norton, 2011), Earthworks (American Philosophical Society Press, 2016), So forth (Norton, 2020), and Max Jacob: A life in art and letters (Norton, 2020). She is also the recipient of numerous awards for her work, including the Lamont Poetry Prize for her second volume, Stained glass (Norton, 1993), and the Witter Bynner Poetry Prize (1994) and Award of Merit Medal for Poetry from the American Academy of Arts and Letters.

prefercite.head: Preferred Citation
prefercite.p: Rosanna Warren Papers. Yale Collection of American Literature, Beinecke Rare Book and Manuscript Library.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: The Rosanna Warren papers consist of subject files, writings, and personal papers that document Warren's professional and personal activities. The bulk of the papers range in date from Warren's youth to the library's acquisition of the papers in 2022. Correspondents include William Arrowsmith, John Ashbery, Saul Bellow, Harold Bloom, Peter Blume, Cleanth Brooks, D. S. Carne-Ross, Eleanor Clark, Donald Davie, Joan Fitzgerald, Robert Fitzgerald, Louise Glück, Linda Gregerson, Seamus Heaney, John Hollander, Rika Lesser, Christopher Middleton, Christopher Ricks, Arnold Stein, Mark Strand, Dorothea Tanning, and Robert Penn Warren.

userestrict.head: Conditions Governing Use
userestrict.p: The Rosanna Warren Papers is the physical property of the Beinecke Rare Book and Manuscript Library, Yale University. Literary rights, including copyright, belong to the authors or their legal heirs and assigns. For further information, consult the appropriate curator.

arrangement.head: Arrangement
arrangement.p: Organized into two subseries: People and Other.

processinfo.head: Processing Information
processinfo.p: The library received two separate runs of alphabetized subject files identified as "Study" and "Old" files. Files have largely been preserved. Files consisting exclusively or primarily of material relating to a piece of writing were shifted to Series II. Writings. Otherwise, the bulk of the files, for named individuals, have been arranged into a single alphabetical run in the "People" subseries.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Series I. Subject Files consists chiefly of files for named individuals (the first subseries). These files tend to be mixed in nature and may include examples or combinations of any of the following: correspondence, drafts or copies of writings by the individual, writings by third parties, photographs, clippings and printed ephemera, and other materials. Some files contain only correspondence or only material relating to the named individual.

In addition to files for Warren's parents, authors Eleanor Clark and Robert Penn Warren, there are files for family members, personal and family friends, and artists, authors, and educators that span Warren's and her parent's generations, thus, for people born between roughly 1900 and 1955. These include William Arrowsmith, John Ashbery, Peter Blume, Cleanth Brooks, D. S. Carne-Ross, Donald Davie, Robert Fitzgerald, Louise Glück, Linda Gregerson, Geoffrey Hill, John Hollander, Rika Lesser, Christopher Middleton, Christopher Ricks, Mark Strand, and Dorothea Tanning, among others.

Warren added handwritten notes on the covers of many folders associated with these subject files. The notes provide context on the origins and/or nature of the personal and professional relationships, some of which were formed during Warren's childhood and while she was living and travelling in Europe in the mid-1960s and early 1970s.

Files for individuals and couples identified specifically as family friends, for example, include Saul Bellow, Bill and Susie Crofut, the sculptor Joan Fitzgerald, John Palmer, a long-time editor of the Yale review , Arnold and Bess Stein, and C. Vann Woodward.
The second subseries includes files relating to other topics, organizations, events, or projects.
Chiefly in English; some materials in French, Italian, and German.

arrangement.head: Arrangement
arrangement.p: Arranged alphabetically by title and folder heading.

processinfo.head: Processing Information
processinfo.p: There are post-it notes attached to scattered drafts and documents in Series II. Most post-it notes are attached near and extend beyond the right edge of the page. Single sheets with post-it notes have been placed in mylar. Some post-it notes are blank and some include handwritten notes by Warren. In cases where the post-its include notes by Warren, preservation photocopies have been made to document the physical location of the post-its on the page, along with their marginal annotations, as they were received by the library. These photocopies have been placed immediately behind the page or document as appropriate.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Series II. contains drafts and other material associated with both book-length projects and shorter writings by Warren.

Warren is the author of several collections of poetry, a book of literary criticism, a biography, and a translation of Euripides' "The Suppliants". Drafts and other materials can be found for the following collections: Departure (Norton, 2003), Each leaf shines separate (Norton, 1984), Earthworks (American Philosophical Society Press, 2016), Ghost in a red hat (Norton, 2011), Snow day (Palaemon Press, 1981), So forth (Norton, 2020), and Stained glass (Norton, 1993). There are also materials relating to Fables of the self: Studies in lyric poetry (Norton, 2008), Max Jacob: A life in art and letters (Norton, 2020), and Suppliant women (Oxford University Press, 1995).

In addition to the drafts and other material relating to book-length works, there are files for shorter writings such as essays, lectures, talks, and tributes. These files often contain various materials associated with a work, including correspondence, research material, notes, drafts, proofs, and printed and other material. Correspondence often documents the context for production or publication of the work, and notes, drafts, and proofs document the evolution of individual works.
Notebooks containing drafts of poems, files containing drafts of individual poems, and loose poems are aggregated under the heading "Poems" in the alphabetized listing of titles.
Additional writings by Warren can be found in Series I. Subject Files.
Chiefly in English; some materials in French and Italian.

arrangement.head: Arrangement
arrangement.p: Organized into four subseries: Notes and Notebooks, Photographs, Printed Material, and Other Material.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Series III. brings together all remaining materials in the Warren papers. The series is subdivided for Notes and notebooks, Photographs, Printed material, and Other material. Notebooks date from an early French language book made by Warren's mother in the early 1960s through to the late 1970s. Chiefly, notebooks date from Warren's years at Milton Academy, a boarding school in Massachusetts, Yale University, where she was an undergraduate during the early to mid-1970s, and from subsequent years in Europe. Some notebooks include drawings.

The photographs subseries is subdivided for albums, commercial packets preserved by Warren, people, places, and other. The oldest album, with captions, includes photographs from Warren's childhood dating circa 1966 to 1971. There are photographs of Rosanna Warren, her brother Gabriel, her parents, Dorothea Tanning, and unidentified others on locations in France, Italy, and Egypt. Other albums consist of photographs of the family home in Fairfield, Connecticut, and an unidentified outdoor location respectively.

The labelled commercial packets, including prints and negatives, overlap in content and dates with the oldest album. There are additional photographs from Egypt, France, Italy, and Milton Academy from the mid-1960s to 1971. In addition to loose photographs of Warren and her immediate family, there are photographs of Katherine Anne Porter, David St. John, and Elizabeth Spires. There are also larger files for Warren and her father with others.

Printed materials feature four titles by Herman Melville from the 1892 edition by United States Book Company. The volumes were gifted by Elizabeth S. Melville to Warren's great-grandfather, Charles Henry Phelps, and handed down through the family. Other printed ephemera includes event posters and publicity for readings and lectures by Warren.
Other materials include materials relating to family members, juvenalia, sketchbooks, and student papers.

accessrestrict.head: Conditions Governing Access
accessrestrict.p: Box 79 (optical disks): Restricted fragile material. Reference copies may be requested. Consult Access Services for further information.
